Welcome to nail the hookpoint. This is a very important module
because we are getting close to getting the deal closing. Now,
we've been together with the audience, for the buyer or the
investor whatever it may be for some good amount of time now.
Let's take a look at the time sequencing just to get an
understanding of how far we've come and where we are. As we
got into setting the frame, we did all those things that are
important to getting control and establishing status. We'll put the
status line in here.
Okay, when we started out we really started from a position of no
power, no strength, no authority, no status, or very limited, so we
worked pretty hard. Because we didn't want to start our pitch to
get up to this status point right here. If you're as good as you can
get, which I may be, takes me about 10 maybe 15 minutes, so I'll
put 10 minutes here. Then we moved into tell the story. Tell the
story, we probably spent, oh, 20 minutes, in telling the story or
giving the pitch and that moved us up to this point of status here.
We know that 20 minutes, we don't want to spend longer on it
because that's the limits of human attention. From there, we
know we've got people pretty tired, we've provided some
counter-pressure at this point, which would make people qualify
themselves to us, tell us why we should let them be our
customer. Let them in our deal.
Then we had to kind of re-boot their brain, get them re-excited.
They just were fatigued and tired, at that point. If you are good at
it, you spend about ten minutes doing that. Then we moved in to
discussing commercial terms. So we moved into offer the prize
and put us at the status right around here. How long do we do
that? Again, if you do a great job at it, about ten minutes. So let's
do a summary. At this point we have been with the buyer or the
audience for 50 minutes. 50 minutes is a long time to hold
somebody's attention. Let's talk through this for a few moments. I
think, here's one way to consider it. While you're listening to
these words right now and watching this screen, millions of
sensory neurons in your brain are going off like blasting caps.
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	1
Oren Klaff:
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 1 of 18 --

Boom, boom, boom. All trying to knock down the wall of attention
as they are trying to get you to focus on something new. Only a
few of these blasting caps or firing neurons will break through to
your conscious awareness and the rest will be ignored. It's easy
enough for you to let some of my messages get through, if you
say, “Hey, I'm really going to pay attention to Oren now, to Pitch
mastery, I've been doing that for a long time.” You can will
yourself to let these messages get through. In the same way, that
right now you are not focusing on your toes that are crammed up
in the front of your shoes, but if you think about them right now,
then you allow some attention and focus to go on them then
suddenly you are aware of them and you can feel them. So you
can choose what you want to let through in terms of attention.
But from me trying to earn your attention fire neurons and get it.
It's much more difficult.
I say this, because where we are now, right here, nailing the
hook-point, I don't think you're going to get a lot more attention
from someone after 50 minutes. You've raised their buying
temperature, as high as it's probably ever going to go. This is a
new subject. I want to explore buying temperature now. Let's
clear the screen, talk about buying temperature. Let's put a
buying temperature line on here so you can get a sense of what
we're talking about. If this line, is the sense of the level of
excitement and interest and passion and attention that
somebody's giving you, enough to which they would say, “Yes, I'm
interested, I'll sign up, I'll buy it, I want the deal, send me the
product, I'll do the joint venture with you, let's go make this thing
happen.” If this line is that level, at which that level of excitement
is high enough. Here's what I see most people do. They'll walk in a
room and they really start pitching. They start to really raise
buying temperature, try and generate a lot of excitement. Okay?
People are good at that. They walk in a room and get the audience
excited and buyer excited and they get the level of excitement
right up here where I'm pointing with this arrow. Okay?
High enough to buy, but not enough has happened. We haven't
given the pitch, they haven't had time to marinade in the pitch,
you haven't controlled emotions, you haven't set the frame
strong enough. So maybe some people are more patient and for
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	2
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 2 of 18 --

them it looks like this. They raise buying temperature someway
midway through the pitch. Get people all frothed up and excited
to buy, the excitement level reaches a tizzy. But it's still too early
to raise somebody's buying temperature. You're never going to
raise somebody's buying temperature above this red line. That's
when they're ready to buy. You can't keep somebody hot and
excited and motivated and interested and passionate perpetually.
You have to reach that point and then close the deal. If you reach
that point before you are here at the hook-point and ready to get
the deal, then you've raised buying temperature too early. Let's
keep looking at this. Maybe, let's say you're pretty good at it and
you've raised buying temperature over time, right into reveal the
intrigue, get people excited and passionate and motivated and
you gave your great pitch and your story was [thing] and you've
set the frame. It's still too early to have buying temperature hit
the point of I'm ready. Because you haven't done enough. You
haven't even offered commercial terms. Haven't even done this
here.
I want to suggest this concept, of controlling buying temperature
in this way. Keep it under control and raise it using the skills we
have all the way through, nail the hook-point. When you set the
frame, it doesn't mean you're not going to go for status. Of course
we want to raise status as high as we can. That looks like this.
Don't want to make this too complicated but we want to get high
status there, we want to get high status there, but buying
temperature we want to raise over time. Buying temperature we
want to raise over time, until we get just past nailing the hook-
point, the buying temperature is here and every thing is in
alignment. Something to think about. You cannot keep someone
perpetually excited to do your deal. They can't do the deal until
you've offered the commercial terms, you've nailed the hook-
point, you've given a great pitch, set the frame, all the stuff you
need to do. I'm going to talk about that now in a little more detail,
but this is an important concept. So important, I'm going to do a
quick review. Many people will come in and they will go to get the
buyer excited. I call that raising buying temperature. They get
them too excited too fast. We haven't given the rest of the pitch
yet. All of this remains to be done. So, why get somebody's buying
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	3
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 3 of 18 --

temperature to the buying point before you've given the whole
pitch. They can't actually buy at that point.
Other people will be a little bit more patient and they will do it this
way. Wait till the end of the pitch or towards the end of the pitch
to get the customer really excited. But even that is too fast.
Others will go like this, and wait even longer, until past the pitch.
But I think for you even that is going to be too fast to raise buying
temperature. I think this is the mode you want to be in is raise it,
perpetually, carefully to nail the hook-point. Now, lets get into
nailing the hook-point, and really define that. You can see in this
shaded area we're now in nail the hook-point, let's clear the
screen. It's time to nail the hook-point. Let's define that. The
hook-point is a singular moment in time, when your audience is
having an immersive experience with you. They are completely
paying attention to you and into the things that you're saying.
They are interested in both you and the subject. They are
intrigued. They are having this immersive experience with you,
and then they start adding value to the deal. That's the hook-
point. How does the buyer or the audience add value? And how do
you recognize that has happened?
It's when they start contributing ways to make the deal stronger,
the relationship better or the transaction easier. Have you ever
been in a deal and just known that the other guy's wanted it? They
were signaling they wanted the deal. But what were those signals
really? At those times when you just knew the buyer was ready to
do the deal, it was because they started making things easier on
you. They started adding value. Here's some examples of real
hookpoints I've experienced with buyers over time. When I was
helping a company sell their software to a large buyer, I heard this
from the buyer. “We're really excited to get this software installed.
Hey, we could introduce you to our suppliers who also need this
software.” When you hear that from your buyer, that they want to
make introductions to other people they know to help you out,
they are adding value and you're probably at the hook-point. I
heard this recently. “Don't worry about getting approval from our
chief financial officer, we'll make that happen, so you don't have
to deal with it.” So at the point, if you've been through strong, set
the frame, tell the story, reveal the intrigue, offer the prize and
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	4
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 4 of 18 --

now you guys are working on actually doing the deal and you hear
“Hey don't worry about talking to our chief financial officer” the
most painful thing you can do in the world, “We'll help make that
happen so you don't have to deal with it” you are at the hook-
point. I heard this very recently from a company I was working
with in Geneva. They were investing one of my deals in Southern
California they said, “Hey. Normally we can only invest $1million in
any deal that's outside of Europe, but we're going to break the
rules here and raise our investment to $3 million, if you'll agree.”
So if you've offered the prize and somebody is saying, “We'll break
the rules to give you more money, we hope you agree with that” I
think that's pretty obvious. You are at the hook-point. Here's
another one that you'll recognize. If your buyer says to you “Hey,
let's spend some social time together, we'd like to take you out to
dinner, to one of the nicest places in town.” When the buyer
wants to take you out to dinner, you're definitely at the hook-
point. I heard this just recently this month. “Hey Oren, if you can
close in two weeks, we'll pay all the legal costs to help make this
happen.” Right? The legal costs in that deal are $50,000. When
somebody's willing to pay the legal costs and trying to make
things easier on you, they're adding value and you are at the
hook-point. These kinds of value adds can be thought of as any
action the buyer takes to sell themselves the deal, doing your job
for you. To negotiate themselves, again, helping you out, making
things easier for you. You'll see lots of times guys will find that's
one of the ways he can easily add value is just to negotiate against
themselves. They will concede important deal points for no
benefit to themselves or they will sell themselves to you
unnecessarily. Explain why they are a good customer, explain how
they are going to help make the transaction easy. Why they are
going to let you make lots of profit and how they are going to buy
more and more stuff in the future.
When the buyer is doing that, they are looking for ways to add
value and these are a lot of the behaviors that you'll see. Where
does this concept of “Value add” come from? Well, imagine that
you are pitching a deal. You are with your audience. What are you
trying to do? Get attention? Yes, of course. Tell a great story? Sure.
Keep it intriguing? Yes. Time constraints, provide fast and visual
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	5
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 5 of 18 --

stimulus, frame control. Yes, yes and yes you are trying to do all
that stuff. But, ultimately you are trying to add value. Trying to
add value to them, to their business and their lives. Remember,
human interactions are all about value add. You have heard this
many times in many ways in your own career, right? “What's your
value add? How do those guys add value? How is this additive? You
do something for me and I'll give you money. You do something
for me and I'll do something for you.” It's all value exchange. If
you have nothing to exchange for value then you are dead in the
water. Nobody does anything for you unless they are receiving
value. Value flows back and forth in all relationships. It's just the
way of things. With people you know, this value flow or value
exchange is really reciprocal. I cook, you do the dishes. I change
the copier toner, you take the checks to the bank. I deal with the
auditors, you go to the damn customer service meeting.
Whatever, it's all reciprocal value exchange.
Want to see how this works really? Stop reciprocating. Just take
value but don't reciprocate it. People will stop liking you very
quickly. Why? It's obvious. Because you are taking value without
adding it in return. Value flows back and forth in all human
relationships and obviously, especially in business relationships. If
you haven't observed this and whatever, you've just landed here
from Mars five minutes ago. Business relationships are value
exchange. You see, with people you don't know, with people you
are pitching, potential JVs or customers or investors, there isn't
this strong reciprocal relationship, “I do the dishes, you cook”
There may not be any reciprocation at all, that's what I find in the
beginning. In the beginning of a relationship, a new business
relationship you have to add almost all of the value. After all, you
are pitching them. Seeking that they do something they weren't
otherwise planning to do. Give you money or sign a contract or
help you out in some way. In the beginning you need to add all the
value. That's what strong is about right? It's getting control of
ways to add value. So you need to set the frame. They don't set
the frame for your benefit. You set the frame, then you have to
tell a great story in 20 minutes. It has to be fast, visual, novel,
concrete, high contrast, you have to do all that. Then after all
that's done, you have to recognize they're tired. You have to
reveal more intrigue. You're adding value. They are not doing
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	6
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 6 of 18 --

anything but just sitting there and responding in ways that you've
set the frame. Then you have to offer the prize, they don't do that.
So you have to do all of that, right up to this point. To me, this is
where it is fascinating. When you have added tons of value and
they are immersed in your experience, they're continuing to be
intrigued, you're a compelling character, your product or your
service or your company is very interesting, they are immersed in
your experience. They reach a tipping point inside themselves.
In their mind, this is what they are thinking. “Hmm, Oren and his
team have pitched this deal, you know, this has been very
interesting. I have taken a lot of value here. I have received a lot.
Oren and his guys are great. They really care, they're considerate
they did the pitch in 20 minutes. They kept it interesting, they
provided insight. They kept the pace going fast. They made their
presentation novel, concrete, visual, high contrast. They created
intrigue. They made us think about things we hadn't thought of
before. They introduced new concepts to us. This has been
colorful and this has been really refreshing. We are really glad we
came to this.” Then what's great is they think this. They are at the
tipping point, they think, “it's time for me to reciprocate. I got a
lot for myself, it's time to add some value to this relationship.” At
that moment, when your buyer gets the gut sense and he says to
himself “It's time for me to reciprocate, I got a lot for myself and
it's time to add some value to the relationship.”
It's at that moment that you have nailed the hook-point. As I have
defined the hook-point, right, I suggested that you have nailed
the hook-point when two things are present. The audience or the
buyer has started to add value. Really you've been through strong,
set the frame, tell the story, reveal the intrigue, offer the prize,
and then they start to add value in some way. Make the
relationship stronger, make the transaction easier or concede
some deal points. They do this while they are immersed in your
presentation. Then you have got to the hook-point. We talked
about value add. Let's talk about immersion for a minute. It's a
very interesting subject. Technically, immersion is a state of
mental focus, so intense that awareness of the outside world is
lost. It generally results in a feeling of happiness, joy, comfort and
certainly satisfaction. Another way to think about this in business,
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	7
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 7 of 18 --

immersion is chemistry. Chemistry is when you just hit it off with
another group or another person. Your anecdotes are funny. Even
the unfunny ones, people laugh at. The body language is great,
people are open, it doesn't matter what you say. It's interpreted
properly. You can say, “Hey guys, you're a good friend” Or you can
say, “Hey guys, you're a fiend” it doesn't matter because they
know what you mean. You are a friend. The audience knows what
you mean. They are syncopated with you in phase, in step. You
guys are using synchronicity, the gears are meshed. You are
completing each other's sentences using extra sensory
perception. You are literally mind reading each other. It's going
great. This is chemistry.
In fact, ad agencies have a whole phase called chemistry
meetings. They literally say, “Hey, we're going to meet Ford or GE
or Kellogg for the chemistry meeting.” This is to evaluate the
chemistry between themselves and the client. This single
meeting, the chemistry meeting, drives how the rest of the
relationship goes. Many times it's the foundation for $100 million
of a business in a large ad agency. When I introduced the idea of
immersion to a very large ad agency that you can manufacture
immersion and you could manufacture chemistry, they got very
excited. Think about it for a minute. Achieving chemistry is having
someone immersed in your experience. It's achieving hot
cognition. We've talked a lot about it. It's taking someone all the
way through strong. It's setting the frame, telling a great story,
revealing the intrigue, offering the prize and then providing an
immersive experience. So talking about getting immersion. The
point of total immersion is providing the environment for the
hook-point to happen. It's having frame control, it's having
properly managed time. To provide novelty. Introducing intrigue,
creating wanting. These are all things we've done up to now.
So the whole point of the strong exercise, the program, is to add
value in an immersive environment and then provide them the
space to add value back to you, and then for you to know you
have reached the hook-point. And, again, you are going to look
for that point when they want to add value, they are going to try
and make the relationship stronger. For example “Hey, we can fly
out to meet you” Right? And you know you are at the hook-point.
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	8
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 8 of 18 --

That's a big effort for a buyer or audience for someone to fly out to
meet you. They want to make the transaction easier. I've heard
this before. “Hey look, let's not wait for legal to sort all this out,
we'll send a check today to get the transaction started.” I once
received a $2.1 million investment before any paperwork was
drafted. That's a good sign that you've reached the hook-point,
when you actually get the money. Another way they're going to
add value which we said before, the terms of the deal get better. I
heard this just last month. “Let's not argue about price anymore.
Let's just make make it $30 million even, and we'll move on and
get this done.”
That's obviously the hook-point. Let's not argue about price let's
just make it a round number that's fair to everyone. That is the
hook-point. And last thought on this. Where do you find
immersion or how do make sure you are creating it? Immersion is
at the crossroads of experiencing and thinking. It's at the center
of feeling and analyzing. It's at the intersection of perception and
cognition. So let's not get choked out on these words, but they
are very important to understand. Let's define a few things so we
can go deeper into this idea of immersion. So how do you get
immersion? The way to get into this subject is really to understand
two things. Perceptual systems, and cognitive systems. I don't like
jargon any more than you do, so let's get through this quickly.
Perceptual systems are really easy. Perception is our senses, it's
what we take in from the world around us. And cognitive systems
are internal systems. It's what we think. It's the frames, cognitive
frames, it's our internal thinking.
Man 1: Perception is the process of receiving information from our
senses and categorizing it inside our minds. Think about your
brain for a moment, it's dark in your skull. There is no direct link
to the outside world. The brain has no ability to feel, see or hear. It
must rely on information from your organs, your eyes, ears and
skin. Perception is the organization, identification and
interpretation of information in order to build an understanding of
what is going on outside the body. All perception involves signals
from the nervous system. Which in turn result from physical
stimulation of the sense organs. For example, vision involves light
striking the retinas of the eye, smell is caused by odor molecules
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	9
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 9 of 18 --

and hearing involves pressure waves. Perception is not the
passive receipt of these signals. Perception is the interpretation of
signals, and the making of meaning. We perceive the world
around us in order to drive actions favorable to survival. You may
believe the purpose of perception is knowledge, but evolutionary
psychologists hold that its primary purpose is to guide action. For
example, depth perception seems to have evolved not to help us
know the distances to other objects, but rather to help us move
around in space. We use eyesight for collision avoidance
suggesting that vision is basically for directing action not
providing knowledge.
Perception is used to help us take action. More than half the brain
is devoted to processing sensory information, and the brain itself
consumes roughly a quarter of your metabolic resources.
Therefore the senses must provide exceptional benefits to fitness
and survival. Perception accurately mirrors the world. Humans get
useful accurate information through their senses so they can take
action. In business, activating the perceptual system, or feeding
the senses new stimuli gives people raw data. But there is a
limited interpretation of this data during perception. There's not
much knowledge happening at all. During perception it's just
“Move, assess environment, survive, move.” Cognition literally
means “To know”. Cognitive processes are de-coupled from your
physical environment. By contrast, you cannot de-couple
perception from your physical environment, but you don't need
new information to think. Knowledge can be thought of as
problem solving and reaching conclusions from the act of
thinking. Before we said the brain is stuck in a black box. There is
no light, sound or smell in there. That's just fine for thinking which
is the job of manipulating previously stored images, sounds,
symbols and emotions. Very simplified, perception creates a data
set for the brain to manipulate. Cognition creates knowledge,
ideas and solutions from this data. The term cognition refers to all
processes by which the sensory input is transformed, reduced,
elaborated, stored, recovered and used. Cognition is thinking, and
thinking can happen even in the absence of new sensory
stimulation.
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	10
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 10 of 18 --

Oren: 	So when perceptual systems and cognitive systems
are under-taxed, people become apathetic and bored. When you
are not giving people a lot of sensory input and you are not
making them think very hard, that's called under-taxing their
perceptual and cognitive systems and your audience becomes
apathetic and bored. Similarly if people are overtaxed, if you've
given too much perceptual or sensory input and you've made
people think too hard, they become overtaxed and that leads to
stress and frustration. So, immersion happens between these two
states, when perceptual and cognitive systems are challenged at
near capacity, without being exceeded. Under these conditions,
the audience loses a sense of the real world, or the outside world.
And they typically experience intense feelings of, as I said before,
joy and satisfaction. Immersion is characterized by the following
four things. You've given your audience challenges that can be
overcome. You've given them a feeling of control over the
situation they are in and how they are learning interesting things.
They have a loss of concern over matters of the self. So they are
not thinking about being thirsty or hungry, and they have a
modified sense of time. Hours pass, and it feels like minutes.
Let's talk about immersion a little bit more in terms of these
concepts. Perceptual inputs and cognitive inputs. Theme park
rides, Six Flags, Great Adventure, whatever, they can be totally
immersive, right? They are overly loaded on the perceptual side.
There is no cognitive or thinking stimulation. It doesn't fall
between the two states. Complex games like chess can be
immersive. But they are completely cognitively loaded. It's all
about thinking. There's no movement, there's no sensory
perception. It's all lost in your mind, thinking about the game you
are in. Both of those are immersive environments but they are not
conducive to creating wanting, or reaching the hook-point. It's
very easy to build environments that achieve immersion through
perceptual stimulation. Those are theme park rides, or going to a
motor cycle or monster truck race, or going to a football game,
right? But those are brief experiences and they are not good for
pitching. The optimal immersive experience involves rich sensory
experiences and rich cognitive engagement. Again, the audience
should experience challenges that can be overcome,
environments that minimize distractions are good for reaching
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	11
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 11 of 18 --

the hook-point, having an immersive experience. Promote a
feeling of control. You see, that's why I hate selling. It takes the
control away from the buyer. They feel constantly like you are
manipulating them.
When you create an immersive environment, you want to
emphasize stimulus that are warm or hot, versus cold and
analytical. Really, you want to help people disconnect from reality
or the outside world for the period of time that you have them,
and think about building a future with you, your product, your
joint venture, your investment. Those are immersive experiences.
Along the way, having done all these things and in the module S,
and module T and R, reveal the intrigue, O offer the prize, there
are many things to do. But I will say it this way, you will need to
have hit certain way-points during the lead up to the hook-point.
Here's a new term.
Way-points are a set of coordinates that identify a point in
physical space.” So, traditionally a way-point is to identify a point
in physical space. Way-points have traditionally been associated
with distinctive features in the real world. Rock formations,
mountains, streams, tree clumps. But today Way-points are
obviously manufactured specifically for navigation. Way-points
are radio beacons, buoys in the ocean, satellites, GPS markers. In
the modern world, the idea of Way-points has become
increasingly abstract. That's what we're going to talk about here.
Often they have no obvious relationship or any distinctive features
in the real world. These abstract Way-points are used to help
define invisible routing paths from navigation. For example,
artificial airways or highways in the sky are created specifically for
the purposes of air navigation. They have no real clear connection
to the features of the real world. Really, highways in the sky or
where airplanes are allowed to go, only consist of a series of
abstract Way-points to which pilots have to navigate. Our airways
which keep us safe when we fly are designed to facilitate air traffic
control and the routing of traffic between heavily traveled
locations only by Way-points and pilots have no reference really
to natural terrain features. Now a definition. A way-point is a fixed
point along a planned course used to make a journey or simple a
point of reference useful for navigation. Now, let's bring it into the
pitch, this concept of Way-points. Nine out of ten times we are
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	12
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 12 of 18 --

only going to get to the hook-point, when we've gone through a
series of cognitive Way-points. Here, we've put up the strong
model, with the status line running through it, and have listed the
14 Way-points. Let's discuss them and look at where they go
chronologically through the pitch.
The big idea of course, is going to be somewhere in this area. It's a
way-point you have to hit. Prizing, you're going to be doing that.
All the way along, setting the frame. Certainly you are going to see
the strongest amount of prizing or counter-pressure, when you
finish giving your pitch. A social engagement. You are going to see
a lot of social engagement along here while you are setting the
frame. Certainly when you are revealing the intrigue. I mean it's
impossible to get to nailing the hook-point without creating fun
and laughter and social engagement. Come on guys, this stuff has
got to be fun for the audience. Why would they be there
otherwise? That's one of our tenets right? We're always having
fun. If we want to have fun, we've got to give it to the audience as
well. Frame control. Somewhere in here you are going to
perpetrate a denial or a defiance and certainly down this way,
when you get to offer the prize, you are going to be getting some
static, some questions, some feedback. You are going to need to
get frame control back again. You are going to do that with denial
and defiance as we've talked about.
Tension. Tension is a way-point you are going to have to go
through. High point of tension here when you apply counter-
pressure. High point of tension here when you offer the prize, and
maybe right when you walk in the room. I like to do it. “Hey guys,
good to meet you. Oh, I see there's a lot of billionaires in this room
and $100 millionaires and I'm very happy to have all of you here.
Let me decide who I am going to kick off the island first. Who
should I ask to leave?” Very tense. So tension is a way-point you
have to go through. You're going to build the character, we know
specifically where you are going to build a compelling character.
It's right there at the beginning of your pitch. If you don't do that,
you haven't passed through that way-point and it's going to be
hard to nail the hook-point later on. Again, when you build the
compelling character it's going to be here. That's a way-point you
have to go through.
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	13
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 13 of 18 --

Novelty and intrigue. It's very powerful. It's nobvious, all the way
through here. That's when you're going to be passing through that
way-point. Giving a clear value proposition. If you have not done
that, it's going to be very hard to nail the hook-point. And you
give a clear value proposition somewhere in here during your
pitch. A generic solution, that's another way-point you've got to
go through. That's obviously going to be somewhere in your
pitch. It's going to be in this area. Crock friendly pitch. Fast, novel,
visual, high contrast and concrete. Think that's a way-point?
That's going to be right in here. Apply counter-pressure. Well we
know exactly where that is, we've discussed it many times. Right
there. We need information along the way of giving a pitch or
making a sale, right. But we want to avoid asking lots of
questions. We make statements that the audience or the buyer or
the investor will engage with. We make statements. So that's
probably in here while we're having a bunch of conversation,
definitely in here. That is a way-point that you will go through. Not
asking questions to gain information. But making interesting
statements and having the audience engage with you. If that has
not happened you have not passed through that way-point. Can't
really get to nail the hook-point.
You need to have suggested you'll walk away if the deal is not
right for you. That probably happened here and here and here and
here and here and here and here and here. If you haven't gone
through that way-point six or seven times, I'll be surprised. Of
course you are going to constrain time. That's a way-point you
will go through. You may have done it as I do, right here. “Hey
guys, I've got about 20 minutes to give you the pitch, we'll have
some time for Q&A, maybe we'll have an hour to spend together,
then I have to bounce. I have another meeting.” Definitely will do
re time constrain there, probably happens here, and here and
here. So that's an easy way-point to go through. Again I'll define
it. A way-point is a fixed point on the planned course used to
make a journey. Simply a point of reference useful for navigation.
So if you want to navigate yourself over here to the hook-point,
you will pass through these 14 Way-points and then there will be
an immersive experience here in the last 10 or 15 minutes of your
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	14
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 14 of 18 --

meeting, and you will be nailing the hook-point. That's where the
chemistry is perfect.
[music to 00:43:44]
Let's continue this conversation about Way-points and we'll
pause for a minute and say this. I did some training recently with
these Navy SEALS out of Las Vegas. Day long combat training, all
very interesting, but I learned a couple things from the exercises
from the Navy SEALs. For business people, this stuff is mainly a
curiosity. Number one, Navy SEALs, their number one objective is
they keep their priorities in order. They know what comes first,
second, third. It's kind of fortune cookie wisdom. But they seem
to apply that. The second thing is, know when to act without
hesitation. Kind of melodramatic in the boardroom I've never felt
like I had to act in one second without hesitation. I mean,
certainly when people say things about my deal that tries to
weaken it, they'll say “we'll close distance and shut it down” But if
you don't react in the first two seconds, it's not life or death.
There's a third thing I found very interesting from this fighting
training with these Navy SEALs and I think it's interesting and
applicable. So these guys, these Navy SEALs they train to
recognize the results of an action they take. For example, if they
kick someone in the nuts, they look for something very specific to
indicate that they've done it properly. What will happen is when
you kick someone in the nuts properly, their arms will fling out
and their butt will kind of sink back. That's the social biology of
being kicked there, because that's our reproductive system. So
our arms fling out to protect that a second blow from happening.
We move our butt backwards to create some distance from
wherever that blow came from.
So our arms fling out and our butt moves backwards. So they look
for that reaction. If they don't see it, they know they haven't
achieved the result. Another example. If they go to break
someone's ankle, not something I ever imagine doing or ever will
need to do, the training was fun, but they go to break somebody's
ankle, they'll look for a very specific reaction. Both hands will grab
the ankle and the victim will double over in a certain way. If they
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	15
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 15 of 18 --

don't see that reaction then they know they didn't get the result
and they will have to go back in and try it again.
This applies to us too. So as you progress through strong, set the
frame, tell the story, reveal the intrigue, offer the prize and nail
the hook-point, and you get to these Way-points, you're going to
be looking for the results of your actions. Let's talk through some
of those. When you have delivered the big idea properly, in my
experience, over hundreds of pitches, here's what you are going
to see as a result. Sudden attention. People are going to glance,
put down their phones, stop texting, they are going to lean
forward, and they are going to nod appreciatively. So when you
really deliver the big idea that's the result that you should see.
When you prize properly, you are not going to see a lot of outward
result from the audience, but there is another result you should
detect. You will feel a strong inner sense of control. You know you
are prizing properly, not when you see something from the
audience, but within you. Because the beast rises and you say,
“Yes. I've got some control over myself. Whatever happens I am
not going to react to the audience, but I know that I've had the
courage and the strength to prize myself, to take the,
commoditize them and say, their money is a commodity. What I
have, my product, my service, myself is what's the prize in this
room.” You'll feel that inner sense of control rise within you and
you'll know that's the result. You've passed through the way-
point.
When you have got social engagement, you've made it fun, it's a
no-brainer right? You are going to see smiling from the audience,
their body language is going to open. I'm not a big body language
guy, I don't want to spend all day evaluating body language, but
it's a no-brainer, right? You get social engagement, people are
having fun, their arms open up, they turn towards you, they are
smiling, nodding appreciatively. When you get frame control,
right? You have perpetrated denial or defiance, the audience or
the buyer is reacting to you it's the very definition of frame
control that we've been through many times. You have frame
control when someone is reacting to you in any way. They have
frame control when you are reacting to them. So when you pass
through the way-point of having gotten frame control, obviously
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	16
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 16 of 18 --

when you laugh, they laugh. When you stand up maybe they
stand up and follow you. When you move to a different part of the
room or draw something on the board their gaze follows you. They
turn. When people are reacting to you, you have frame control
and you know you have gone through that way-point. Tension.
When you have created tension what you will see is, you will get a
lot of attention. Focus will be back on you. You have recaptured
attention, that is the result you will get. Having gone through the
tension way-point. When you build a character, shown that
you've done something important, it was difficult, required
difficult decisions in high-stakes circumstances, I'm not sure
there's a result you can measure from the audience. They should
look, continue to look interested. That one's hard, but it's still a
way-point you have to go through.
When you correctly go through the way-point of mixing novelty
and intrigue, again you will have captured attention. People will,
their body will turn toward you, their gaze will look more towards
your face. Not a big body language guy, as we've said, but you'll
feel attention when it's on you. When you deliver a clear value
proposition, you go through the way-point in your pitch, when
you're telling the story, giving the pitch, of giving a clear value
proposition. When you get through that it will feel like you've just
weaved through three basketball players on a difficult course and
dunked the ball. You will have a great self-appreciation. You may
not see a great reaction from the audience, but again, it will well
up inside you, a sense of self-appreciation. Feel like you have
done something great. You will feel it. When you give a clear value
proposition in front of an audience there will be a reaction from
within you. When you give a crock friendly pitch, fast, novel,
visual, high-contrast and concrete and you pass through that
way-point, there will be lots and lots of attentional cues from the
audience. They will turn towards you, they will open up their body
language, they will lean forward. They will submit interesting
comments that support the conversation and they may ask some
questions that are non-generic, that really show that they were
listening to you and are interested.
When you suggest that you will walk away from the deal if it's not
good for you, that's a way-point you have to go through, you
N: Nail the Hookpoint
© 2012 Pitch Anything. All rights reserved.! ! 	! 	! 	! 	! 	17
www.getwsodownload.com

-- 17 of 18 --

might go through that several times. When you suggest that you'll
walk away if it doesn't benefit you, if they're not a good customer
if they are not a good buyer, if they're not going to be a good
investor. You will see two things happening. One it will introduce
tension into the room, get the focus on you at the same time you
will feel an internal self-satisfaction. You have prized yourself. You
have enough backbone to say, “If this deal isn't good for me, I'm
going to walk.” So those are some of the results you'll look for and
in the work book we'll go deeper into that but you'll know exactly
what to look for when you go through these Way-points. That way
you know you've achieved the result. If you don't see the result
you'd better find a way to go through it again. Because if you want
to nail the hook-point, have awesome chemistry, provide an
immersive experience just before you get the deal and the thing
locks down, these are some of the Way-points you have to go
through.
N: Nail the Hookpoint
